answers.value: Approximately $150 per month (about $1,800 per year) from occasional weekend babysitting for a neighbor’s children (ages 4 and 7). The work is irregular and depends on when the parents both work evening shifts at the hospital, typically 2–3 Saturdays per month for 4–5 hours at $15/hour, paid in cash or via Venmo. This income is not guaranteed and often decreases when I am not feeling well enough to watch the children safely. I do not have any rental income, investment income, or alimony/child support. I also receive about $110 per month in SNAP benefits for groceries; I understand this is not technically "income," but I am including it here for full transparency about resources available to my household.

answers.field_label: Other Health Care Expenses Amounts And How Often The Cost Occurs
answers.value:
I have several ongoing health care expenses in addition to what insurance covers, and they have become a significant burden on my household budget:

1. Oncology and specialist copays: I see my oncologist approximately once a month and a hematology nurse practitioner or infusion nurse more frequently during active treatment cycles. Each specialist visit typically has a $45 copay. On average I have 2–3 billable visits per month (oncology follow-up, lab review, and occasional urgent visits related to side effects), which totals about $90–$135 per month in copays.

2. Prescription medications: I am currently on several medications related to my cancer treatment and side effect management (anti-nausea medication, pain management, a proton pump inhibitor for reflux, and a few supportive medications). After insurance, I still pay around $85–$120 per month out of pocket, depending on whether I have to refill everything in the same month. A typical month averages about $100 in prescription copays.

3. Over-the-counter medications and supplies: I regularly purchase over-the-counter items such as acetaminophen, stool softeners, fiber supplements, electrolyte drinks, moisturizing creams for skin affected by treatment, and basic first-aid supplies. These are not covered by insurance. This averages approximately $40–$60 per month; I would estimate about $50 per month on average.

4. Transportation to medical appointments: I do not feel well enough to drive myself on some treatment days, and the hospital is about 18–20 minutes away. I alternate between asking my sister to drive (I contribute gas money when I can) and using rideshare services. I estimate I spend about $60–$80 per month on gas contributions and rideshare fares combined, averaging around $70 per month. During more intensive treatment weeks, this can increase if I have multiple appointments in one week.

5. Counseling/mental health support: I see an oncology social worker through the hospital at no charge, but I also try to see an outside therapist who specializes in chronic illness once or twice a month when I can afford it. My insurance covers part of this, but I still owe a $30 copay per visit. When I am able to go twice a month, this is about $60 per month; in months when finances are tight, I often have to cancel sessions.

6. Past-due medical bills and payment plans: I currently have several older medical bills from imaging studies, emergency room visits prior to my current diagnosis being fully established, and prior lab work. I have arranged payment plans with the hospital and a separate imaging center. I pay $75 per month toward a hospital payment plan and $40 per month toward the imaging center, for a total of $115 per month. I am trying very hard not to fall behind on these, but it is becoming increasingly difficult with my reduced income.

7. Durable medical equipment and supplies: I occasionally need items such as a blood pressure cuff (already purchased), heating pads, and specialized pillows or supports to help with pain and sleeping positions. While these are not recurring monthly charges, they have been intermittent expenses of $30–$60 at a time. Spread over the year, I would estimate this averages around $10–$15 per month.

Altogether, when I add up the consistent monthly health-related costs (copays, prescriptions, transportation, counseling copays, and payment plans), I am spending approximately $440–$480 per month directly on health care, not counting food, rent, utilities, or other basic living expenses. This is a significant portion of my limited household income and is the main reason I am applying for financial support through this program.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form
